1: A revelation of God: 'After this, Job opened his mouth and cursed the day he was born.' The move from silent suffering to voiced despair marks the beginning of the poetic dialogue. Job's curse of his own birth represents the maximum darkness of fallen human experience — the wish for non-existence. Christ does not curse the day of his birth (John 18:37 'for this reason I was born'), but he enters into the reality of those who do (Hebrews 4:15 'tempted in every way, just as we are').
2: The theme: 'Job spoke and said' — the opening of his first lamentation speech. The literary device of the wisdom dialogue begins here; Job's speeches will become progressively more theologically daring until the divine answer. Christ's own cry from the cross (Matthew 27:46 'my God, my God, why have you forsaken me?') is the apex of innocent lament taken up by God himself.
3: The theme: 'Let the day on which I was born perish, and the night that announced, a boy has been conceived.' The opposite of the Annunciation — where Gabriel announces a birth with 'you will conceive and give birth to a son' (Luke 1:31), and heaven celebrates (Luke 2:10-14). Job wishes away the announcement of his birth; the angelic announcement of Christ's birth is the proclamation of humanity's rescue.
4: The theme: 'Let that day be turned to darkness. May God above not care for it, nor light shine upon it.' The theological inverse of Genesis 1:3 ('God said, let there be light'). Job wishes the re-primordial darkness of uncreation upon the day of his birth. The NT's eschatological promise is the final reversal of this darkness: Revelation 21:23 'the city does not need the sun or the moon... for the glory of God gives it light, and the Lamb is its lamp.'
5: The theme: 'May darkness and deep shadow (ṣalmāwet) reclaim it.' ṣalmāwet (shadow of death/deep darkness) is Job's signature darkness-term (Job 3:5; Job 10:21-22; Job 12:22; Job 16:16; Job 24:17; Job 28:3; Job 34:22; Job 38:17). The NT applies this darkness to the condition before Christ: Luke 1:79 'to shine on those living in darkness and in the shadow of death (σκιᾷ θανάτου)' — quoting Isaiah 9:2, the Messianic promise. Christ is the light that pierces ṣalmāwet.
6: The theme: 'Let pitch darkness take it; let it not be counted among the days of the year.' The wish for erasure from the calendar of time. The NT vision of time's redemption goes in the opposite direction: every day is redeemed by Christ's presence (Ephesians 5:16 'making the most of every opportunity, because the days are evil'). Time itself is reclaimed rather than cursed.
7: The theme: 'Let that night be barren; let no sound of joy enter it.' The barren night without joy is the antithesis of the song of the angels at Christ's birth (Luke 2:14 'glory to God in the highest heaven, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests'). Job's darkness-wish is inverted by the Incarnation's light-proclamation.
8: A shadow: 'Let those who are skilled at cursing days put a curse on it — those who know how to rouse Leviathan.' Leviathan (lĕwiyāṯān) appears throughout Job (Job 3:8; Job 41:1) as the chaos monster. Revelation 12:9 and 20:2 identify the great dragon/serpent (ὁ δράκων ὁ μέγας) as the ancient serpent, the devil. Christ's conquest of the dragon/Leviathan is the eschatological answer to Job's invocation of the chaos-cursers.
9: The theme: 'May it wait for light that never comes; may it never see the breaking of morning.' The desperate non-dawn anticipates John 1:5 ('the light shines in the darkness') and Job 8:12 ('I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will never walk in darkness but will have the light of life'). The morning that never comes for Job's cursed day does come in Christ — the resurrection morning that breaks the permanent darkness.
10: The theme: 'Because it did not close the doors of my mother's womb to me, nor hide trouble from my eyes.' Job wishes he had never seen — the ultimate recoil from experience. The NT does not promise the avoidance of trouble but transformation through it: John 16:33 'in this world you will have trouble. But take heart! I have overcome the world.' Christ has seen the trouble and overcome it.
11: The theme: 'Why did I not die at birth?' — Job's first death-wish, the wish for non-existence over existence-with-suffering. Hebrews 2:9 'he tasted death for everyone' — Christ takes on what Job wishes had happened to him, turning death from a wish for escape into an accomplished redemption.
12: The theme: 'Why were there knees to receive me, and breasts for me to nurse?' — the received-and-nurtured infant as an image of unwanted gift. Luke 2:22-24 presents the infant Christ at the Temple, received with Simeon's arms (v28) — the welcoming reception of the child who comes as God's gift to the world, the anti-type of Job's unwanted birth.
13: The theme: 'For then I would have lain down and been at rest' — the rest of non-existence that Job desires. The NT redirects this longing: Hebrews 4:9-10 'there remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God; for anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their works, just as God did from his.' The true rest is not in non-existence but in the completed work of Christ.
14: The theme: 'with kings and counselors of the earth who built themselves ruins' — the shared rest of the great with the forgotten. The leveling of status in death is the OT's recognition of human equality before mortality. James 2:1-4 applies the same leveling to Christ's assembly; Revelation 7:9-10 shows the great multitude of every nation, tribe, and tongue equally before the throne.
15: The theme: 'or with princes who hoarded gold and filled their mansions with silver.' The futility of accumulated wealth when death is the leveler. Matthew 6:19-21 ('do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth') and Luke 12:16-21 (the rich fool) apply this OT wisdom. Christ alone offers treasure that death cannot level (Matthew 6:20 'store up for yourselves treasures in heaven').
16: The shadow: 'Or why was I not like a stillborn hidden away, like infants who never saw the light of day?' The stillborn hidden away — the untimely death that never saw light — anticipates the NT's use of premature death as a metaphor for being dead in sin before Christ: Ephesians 2:1-5 'as for you, you were dead in your transgressions and sins... God... made us alive with Christ.' The spiritual stillborn who sees no light is given life through Christ.
17: A revelation of God: 'There the wicked cease from their turmoil, and there the exhausted find rest.' The vision of Sheol as rest from labor. Revelation 14:13 appropriates exactly this: 'blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on... they will rest from their labor, for their deeds will follow them.' The rest that Job imagines in death is redeemed in Christ into resurrection rest.
18: The theme: 'There the prisoners rest together; they no longer hear the taskmaster's shout.' The end of oppression in death. Christ proclaims release to prisoners (Luke 4:18, quoting Isaiah 61:1) and breaks the power of the last taskmaster — death — through his resurrection. The rest that Job imagines is real in Christ, but it comes through resurrection, not dissolution.
19: The theme: 'The lowly and the great are alike there, and the slave is free from his master.' Death's leveling is the OT observation; the NT reframes it through resurrection: Galatians 3:28 'there is neither Jew nor Gentile, neither slave nor free, nor is there male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus.' The equality that death produces by ending everything is surpassed by the equality Christ creates by elevating everything.
20: A revelation of God: 'Why is light given to the one who suffers, and life to those whose souls are bitter?' The theodicy question in its starkest form — why does God sustain life when life means suffering? John 1:4 'in him was life, and that life was the light of all mankind' — the NT answer is that God gives light through the person of Christ, not as an abstract gift but as an incarnate presence who shares the suffering light is given into.
21: The theme: 'those who long for death that does not come, who search for it more eagerly than for buried treasure.' The desperate longing for death that remains unfulfilled is the condition of deep spiritual desolation. The NT response is 1 Corinthians 15:55 'where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?' — the sting of death (the fear that makes it desirable as escape) is removed by Christ's resurrection, transforming death from the desired exit into the defeated enemy.
22: The theme: 'who would be overjoyed and exult when they finally reach the grave.' The perverse joy at finding death is the maximum inversion of the gospel's invitation. Philippians 4:4 'rejoice in the Lord always; I will say it again: rejoice!' — the NT places joy not in reaching the grave but in belonging to the risen Christ who has conquered the grave.
23: A type: 'Why is life given to a man whose way is hidden, whom God has fenced in (sāḵaḵ) on every side?' Job uses the Adversary's own vocabulary (the śĕyāg/hedge of Job 1:10) now as complaint: the divine encirclement experienced as imprisonment rather than protection. Christ is 'fenced in' to human limitation by the Incarnation (Galatians 4:4 'born of woman, born under the law') — the omnipotent Son enclosed in the restrictions of mortality for redemptive purpose.
24: The theme: 'my groaning comes even before my food' — grief displacing appetite. The Psalms of lament use the same image: Psalms 102:4-5 'I forget to eat my food... I am reduced to skin and bones.' Christ identifies with those who grieve and fast: Matthew 6:16-18 affirms fasting as a genuine spiritual discipline; the Gethsemane night is itself an extended experience of grief overwhelming the body.
25: A revelation of God: 'What I dreaded most has befallen me; what I feared has come upon me.' The realization of the worst fear is the human experience that Christ enters fully. Hebrews 5:7 'during the days of Jesus's life on earth, he offered up prayers and petitions with fervent cries and tears to the one who could save him from death, and he was heard because of his reverent submission.' The worst fear — abandonment in death — is precisely what Christ faces at Calvary (Matthew 27:46).
26: The theme: 'I had no ease, no rest, no peace — yet trouble still came.' The cumulative deprivation — no ease (šalwāh), no rest (šāqaṭ), no peace (šāqaṭ) — describes the pre-gospel human condition without divine peace. The NT reverses this threefold absence: John 14:27 'Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you'; Philippians 4:7 'the peace of God, which transcends all understanding, will guard your hearts and minds in Christ Jesus.'
